sections.text: Welcome to Biology, Module Two — Cell Structure and Function. Last lesson we built the chemistry. Now we put that chemistry inside a container that can keep itself alive. Today you learn how cells are organized, and how each tiny part — each organelle — does one job that helps the whole cell stay running.

Right now, about thirty seven trillion cells are working inside you. Some are reading instructions, some are folding proteins, some are dumping waste. You are not one body — you are a city of cells, each one running a tiny factory. The blueprint for that factory is what we are about to open.

Game plan. First, compare prokaryotic cells and eukaryotic cells — same idea, different floor plan. Second, name the big organelles you must know and what each one does. Third, trace a protein from instruction to delivery, using three organelles working together. Two pauses, one trap, one recap, and a clear assignment at the end.

Two main floor plans. Prokaryotic cells — bacteria — keep everything in one open room. DNA floats free, no nucleus, no membrane-bound organelles. Eukaryotic cells — plants, animals, fungi — are bigger and built like a building. DNA is locked in a nucleus, and the rest of the work happens in separate rooms called organelles.

Lock these six organelles into memory. Nucleus stores the DNA blueprint. Ribosomes read that blueprint and build proteins. The rough endoplasmic reticulum folds and ships those proteins. The Golgi apparatus sorts and packages them. Mitochondria turn food into usable energy. The cell membrane decides what comes in and what goes out. Six rooms, six jobs.

Picture the cell as a factory. The nucleus is the design office where the blueprint is stored. Ribosomes are assembly machines. The rough ER is the production line that folds the product. The Golgi is shipping and labeling. Mitochondria are the power station. The membrane is the security gate. One claim — one room, one job.

Here is the trap. Many students think ribosomes are membrane-bound organelles like the nucleus or Golgi. They are not. Ribosomes are tiny molecular machines made of RNA and protein — no membrane around them. They sit free in the cytoplasm or stick to the rough ER. The cell still counts on them, but the membrane rule does not apply.

Your turn. Match each organelle to its job. Organelles: nucleus, ribosome, rough ER, Golgi, mitochondrion, cell membrane. Jobs: package and label, store the DNA blueprint, make energy, control entry and exit, build proteins, fold and ship proteins. Pause now. Write six rows. Press play when you are ready.

Time. Nucleus stores the DNA blueprint. Ribosomes build proteins by reading that blueprint. Rough ER folds and ships those proteins along the production line. Golgi packages and labels them for delivery. Mitochondria make energy from food. The cell membrane controls what enters and leaves. Six organelles, six jobs — one row each, every time.

Now the powerful idea. Three organelles work together to make and move a protein. First the nucleus releases an instruction copy. Next a ribosome on the rough ER reads it and builds the protein. Then the Golgi takes that protein, sorts it, and ships it in a vesicle to where the cell needs it. Three organelles, one chain.

Pause and try. The cell needs to send a protein out through the membrane. Put these four steps in order: ribosome builds the protein, Golgi packages it, nucleus releases the instructions, vesicle delivers it to the membrane. Write the order one to four. Press play when you are ready.

Time. Step one: nucleus releases the instructions. Step two: a ribosome on the rough ER reads those instructions and builds the protein. Step three: the Golgi packages and labels the protein. Step four: a vesicle carries the protein to the cell membrane, where it leaves the cell. Instruction, build, package, deliver.

Recap. Prokaryotic cells are one open room. Eukaryotic cells split the work into organelles. Six to remember: nucleus, ribosome, rough ER, Golgi, mitochondrion, cell membrane — one job each. Ribosomes have no membrane, so do not group them with the others. Protein chain: instruction, build, package, deliver. Structure supports function.

How to actually master this module. Read OpenStax Biology Chapter Four — Cell Structure. Run the Khan Academy practice on cell structures. In your Learn Portal dashboard, complete the assignment: draw and label a eukaryotic cell with ten organelles, and trace how three of them work together to move a protein. Book an advisor check-in if either pause felt shaky. Next up — Module Three.
